title: Fixing Git Commit Message Validation for Long Messages

statement.markdown: Write a function that validates the length of a Git commit message. The function should accept a string as input and return true if the message is valid (between 1 and 50 characters) and false otherwise. However, the function currently has a logical bug that prevents it from correctly handling long commit messages.
statement.inputFormat: A string representing the Git commit message
statement.outputFormat: A boolean indicating whether the commit message is valid
statement.notes: The function should only return true for commit messages between 1 and 50 characters.

examples.input: This is a valid commit message
examples.explanation: The commit message is between 1 and 50 characters

examples.input: This is a very very very very very long commit message that should not be valid
examples.explanation: The commit message is longer than 50 characters

examples.input: This is an invalid commit message
examples.explanation: The commit message is shorter than 1 character

hints.text: Consider using a simple string length check

editorial.title: Editorial: Fixing Git Commit Message Validation
editorial.intuition: The function should check if the commit message is between 1 and 50 characters.
editorial.bruteForce: You can use a simple if-else statement to check the length of the commit message.
editorial.optimalSolution: Use a conditional statement to check if the commit message length is within the valid range.
editorial.proofOfCorrectness: The function should return true for commit messages between 1 and 50 characters and false otherwise.
editorial.dryRun: Test the function with different commit messages to ensure it is working correctly.
commonMistakes: Not checking the length of the commit message
Returning true for invalid commit messages

testCases.input: This is a valid commit message
This is a very very very very very long commit message that should not be valid
This is an invalid commit message
